metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `resolve_session_id()` actually exists in the codebase (grep `src/` for the symbol) before relying on the S1/6014 OUT-scope exclusion; if it is absent, either land S1 first or bring the resolver into scope for this story.
findings.finding: `resolve_session_id()` is asserted to have 'already landed as this stack's base' (story 6014 / S1), but a full repo-wide search finds zero occurrences of `resolve_session_id` anywhere under `src/`. The OUT-scope exclusion 'The shared resolver itself is story 6014 (already landed as this stack's base)' rests on this unverified premise. If the resolver has not shipped, both the write stamp (`_stamp_session`) and the AC proof-commands will fail at import time.
findings.location: Scope section — OUT clause: 'The shared resolver itself is story 6014 (already landed as this stack's base)'; Depends on section: 'S1 (shared resolver resolve_session_id) — landed as the base of this stack.'
findings.suggested_fix: Run `grep -r 'resolve_session_id' src/` before merging this plan. If the result is empty, either (a) rebase this story on top of the landed S1 commit, or (b) remove the OUT exclusion and include `resolve_session_id()` implementation in this story's scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `src/rebar/_commands/session_id.py` and `resolve_session_id()` are present (story 6014 closed) before beginning implementation, OR add a concrete fallback definition if this story is intended to carry that code forward.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_commands/session_id.py` and the `resolve_session_id()` symbol it exports do not exist in the repo; story 6014 (the stated base) is still `in_progress`, not landed. The plan's `_stamp_session` helper invokes a function that is not yet present, making `session_id.py` a hallucinated/premature edit target dependency.
findings.location: What section — 'Write (src/rebar/_commands/txn.py)' / 'Depends on: S1 (shared resolver resolve_session_id) — landed as the base of this stack.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `resolve_session_id()` (from `src/rebar/_commands/session_id.py`, story 6014) is actually present in the repo before merging this plan; if S1 has not landed, either add the resolver to this story's scope or gate this story on S1 completion.
findings.finding: `resolve_session_id()` is referenced as an already-landed dependency (story 6014 / S1), but a full repo-wide grep of `src/` finds zero occurrences of the function anywhere in source code. The `_stamp_session` helper and all five AC proof-commands depend on importing this symbol; without it every test fails at import time before exercising any new logic.
findings.location: What section — '_stamp_session(status_data) helper; … call the shared resolve_session_id() (story 6014)'; Depends on section — 'S1 (shared resolver resolve_session_id) — landed as the base of this stack.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pre-condition check: confirm `src/rebar/_commands/session_id.py` exists and exports `resolve_session_id` before treating S1 as 'landed'. If it has not landed, add it to this story's IN scope or add an explicit blocking dep check to the ACs (e.g., `pytest tests/unit/test_session_id.py -q` as a required pre-flight).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend an existing one) that transitions an already-`in_progress` ticket to a later status and asserts `claimed_session` remains present and unchanged in compiled state.
findings.finding: The plan body describes a persistence invariant — 'The value persists thereafter (mirrors assignee); it is not cleared on later transitions' — but no acceptance criterion verifies this behavior. This is a described deliverable without a covering test.
findings.location: What section (reducer fold paragraph, last sentence) vs. Acceptance Criteria (AC1–AC5)
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'Persistence: after a `claimed_session` is written, a subsequent STATUS transition (e.g. `in_progress -> done`) leaves `claimed_session` intact in compiled state. Proof: `pytest tests/unit/test_claimed_session.py -k persist -q`.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting: (a) the omit-when-None / byte-identical contract, (b) the fork-winner gating rule (fold only in apply branches, never the losing branch), and (c) the persistence-mirrors-assignee decision for `claimed_session`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel cross-component architectural pattern (session provenance stamped on STATUS events, folded into compiled state, with fork-winner gating and a persistence contract mirroring `assignee`) but names no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to capture the decisions.
findings.location: ## What — both the Write and Fold subsections; ## Why ("epic advisory G6/T8")
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (or a `## Design notes` block in CLAUDE.md) covering the three invariants before the PR merges. Reference it from a comment in `_fold_claimed_session` so the code points to the rationale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the re-open/re-claim lifecycle: define whether `_fold_claimed_session` should CLEAR `claimed_session` (or set it to `null`) when a new `open->in_progress` STATUS event is applied that carries NO `session` key, and add an AC covering this case.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly says `claimed_session` is 'not cleared on later transitions', but does not specify what happens when a ticket is re-opened and then re-claimed WITHOUT a session env var. In that scenario the prior `claimed_session` persists in compiled state, falsely associating the current in_progress slot with a past (now-stale) session — a silent lifecycle inconsistency with no defined update/retire rule.
findings.location: What section — Fold paragraph: 'The value persists thereafter (mirrors assignee); it is not cleared on later transitions.'
findings.suggested_fix: In `_fold_claimed_session`, add an else-branch: when the applied status is `in_progress` and `session` is NOT present in `data`, do `state.pop('claimed_session', None)` so a session-less re-claim always reflects reality. Add an AC: 'A re-open followed by a bare re-claim with no session env var leaves `claimed_session` absent from compiled state.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/reducer/_state.py` (`make_initial_state`) to the edit set and declare `"claimed_session": None` in the initial state dict, consistent with the `assignee` analogy the plan cites.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/reducer/_state.py` (`make_initial_state`) is not listed as an edit target despite the plan introducing a new compiled-state key `claimed_session` described as mirroring `assignee`, which IS present in `make_initial_state()`. Omitting `claimed_session` from the initial state schema can cause snapshot/compaction replay inconsistencies and subtle bugs in consumers that distinguish key-absence from `None`.
findings.location: What section — 'Fold (src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py)' — 'The value persists thereafter (mirrors assignee)'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
